news_summary: Advancements in AI safety, quantum computing, cybersecurity, and improved scientific replication are driving transformative progress across technology and research fields.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées en matière de sécurité de l'IA, d'informatique quantique, de cybersécurité et d'amélioration de la reproductibilité scientifique sont à l'origine de progrès transformateurs dans les domaines de la technologie et de la recherche.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de seguridad de la inteligencia artificial, la computación cuántica, la ciberseguridad y la mejora de la replicabilidad científica están impulsando un progreso transformador en los ámbitos de la tecnología y la investigación.

articles.title: A global capital for AI safety is emerging — and it's not in Silicon Valley
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01389-9The United Kingdom is gaining influence through its growing AI-safety ecosystem.
articles.content:
Technology companies such as Google have set up major offices in London.Credit: Tim Robberts/Getty

When Mythos, an artificial-intelligence model from US firm Anthropic, launched to a limited group of companies earlier this year, rumours quickly circulated about the system’s hacking capabilities and threats to cybersecurity. Real clarity about the model’s power came only once the AI Security Institute (AISI) — a UK government-backed organization in London — had evaluated the model.

London, particularly its technology quarter in King’s Cross, is emerging as a key hub outside the United States for AI firms (see ‘King’s Cross hub’), with Anthropic and OpenAI, both headquartered in San Francisco, California, opening major offices there. And the city’s approach has a distinct flavour to that of its international counterparts. Rather than competing with the United States and China on generating ever bigger and more-powerful models, the United Kingdom is carving out a focus on AI safety and understanding the risks of emerging models. AISI — which vets frontier models that companies submit to it on a voluntary basis — is one of several UK AI safety laboratories operating globally.

“The UK has been out ahead in thinking about ethics, responsible AI and AI governance for quite a while,” says David Leslie, who researches ethics, technology and society at Queen Mary University of London.

The London-based safety ecosystem also includes Apollo Research, a lab that has carried out high-profile research into the potential for models to ‘scheme’ against human users, and non-profit organization GovAI, formerly the Centre for the Governance of AI, which has co-authored influential research, such as proposing using access to computing resources as a way to govern AI.

Academic bodies such as University College London, the Alan Turing Institute and the University of Oxford are also nearby, providing AI research expertise and graduate talent.

Although a focus on AI safety is proving to be a successful niche for the United Kingdom, some researchers highlight that AISI is not a substitute for a regulator. Others are also concerned that a narrowing definition of AI safety has become too focused on keeping models under control and secure from attacks, while paying less attention to wider societal impacts such as transparency and the consequences of widespread adoption.

London calling

London’s broader AI ecosystem has been built around DeepMind, the AI firm founded in the city in 2010 and acquired by Google in 2014. Its co-founder, Demis Hassabis, ensured that the firm stayed in London despite the sale. Several of its alumni have founded major start-ups in the city, including Ineffable Intelligence, established by AI researcher David Silver, which earlier this year raised US$1.1 billion in seed funding.

Being in the United Kingdom “gives us a bit more space to think through things in a slightly different way than if you’re in the wheelhouse the whole time”, said Hassabis, speaking to Nature in 2025. Although “hype culture” is growing in the UK AI community, “there’s always been a more measured and an evidence-based approach to evaluating science”, adds Leslie.

The UK government is trying to entice foreign companies by pitching the country as more stable than the United States and more pro-innovation than the European Union. In March, after Anthropic became embroiled in a spat with the US Department of Defense over ethics, London’s mayor Sadiq Khan wrote to the company applauding its stance and inviting it to expand in London, which the firm did the following month. Tech firms see the United Kingdom as being more “amenable to their interests than the EU” is, which has the relatively strict EU AI Act, says Leslie.

“The big tech companies feel as though they have not only a rich environment to build from in terms of intellectual talent, but they’ve got a very agreeable regulatory environment and a very agreeable government environment” in London, he says.

City planning around King’s Cross station in London has transformed the area into Europe’s premier artificial-intelligence cluster.Credit: Michael Szebor/Nature

Safety first

When it comes to efforts to chart the risks of AI, London’s standout resident is the AISI. It was launched in 2023 by former prime minister Rishi Sunak at the first global AI Safety summit, held at Bletchley Park in Milton Keynes, UK, when the country was at the forefront of an international push for AI safety.

It came out of a “really big belief in governments needing to understand what is going on” at the AI frontier, says Jade Leung, the organization’s chief technology officer. AISI performs industry-wide evaluations, such as of how reliably models act as intended or are able to persuade the public. Firms such as Google DeepMind, OpenAI and Microsoft also submit their models for testing, for example to check their capabilities in creating biological weapons. AISI researchers try breaking down the model’s safeguards, so that the firm can improve them.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the UK, particularly London, emerging as a global hub for AI safety, focusing on ethical AI development and risk mitigation. This represents a significant positive impact on society by promoting responsible AI innovation and governance, with broad implications beyond a niche audience. The story provides detailed context about institutions, government involvement, and the ecosystem supporting AI safety, illustrating a meaningful and inspiring development in technology.
articles.personality_title: London grows as a global center for AI safety and ethics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming more powerful and widespread, raising new questions about safety and risks. While the United States and China focus on creating bigger AI models, the United Kingdom is concentrating on making sure AI is safe and ethical.

**What happened** – London, especially the King’s Cross area, is turning into a major hub for AI safety. The UK government supports the AI Security Institute (AISI), which checks new AI models from companies like Anthropic and OpenAI to find risks and improve safety. London also hosts research labs and universities that study AI ethics and governance.

**Impact** – This focus on safety helps prevent harmful uses of AI, such as hacking or creating dangerous tools. London’s approach offers a different way to develop AI, emphasizing careful testing and responsibility. Big tech companies appreciate the UK’s supportive environment, which combines strong research with balanced rules.

**What's next step** – The UK will continue to attract AI firms to expand their safety work in London. Researchers hope to broaden AI safety efforts to include social effects like transparency and fairness. The government and organizations like AISI will keep testing and improving AI models to reduce risks.

**One-sentence takeaway** – London is becoming a key global center for making AI safer and more ethical, bringing together experts, companies, and government support to guide responsible AI development.
articles.personality_title_fr: Londres devient un centre mondial pour la sécurité et l’éthique de l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) devient de plus en plus puissante et répandue, ce qui pose de nouvelles questions sur la sécurité et les risques. Alors que les États-Unis et la Chine créent des modèles d’IA plus grands, le Royaume-Uni se concentre sur la sécurité et l’éthique de l’IA.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Londres, surtout le quartier de King’s Cross, devient un centre important pour la sécurité de l’IA. Le gouvernement britannique soutient l’AI Security Institute (AISI), qui évalue les nouveaux modèles d’IA de sociétés comme Anthropic et OpenAI pour détecter les risques et améliorer la sécurité. Londres accueille aussi des laboratoires et des universités qui étudient l’éthique et la gouvernance de l’IA.

**Impact** – Cette approche aide à prévenir les usages dangereux de l’IA, comme le piratage ou la création d’armes. L’approche de Londres offre une autre façon de développer l’IA, en mettant l’accent sur des tests rigoureux et la responsabilité. Les grandes entreprises technologiques apprécient l’environnement favorable du Royaume-Uni, qui allie recherche solide et règles équilibrées.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Royaume-Uni continuera d’attirer des entreprises d’IA pour étendre leur travail en sécurité à Londres. Les chercheurs espèrent élargir la sécurité de l’IA aux effets sociaux comme la transparence et l’équité. Le gouvernement et des organisations comme l’AISI continueront de tester et d’améliorer les modèles d’IA pour réduire les risques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Londres devient un centre mondial clé pour rendre l’IA plus sûre et éthique, réunissant experts, entreprises et soutien gouvernemental pour guider un développement responsable de l’IA.
articles.personality_title_es: Londres se convierte en un centro mundial para la seguridad y ética de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) se está volviendo más poderosa y común, lo que genera nuevas preguntas sobre su seguridad y riesgos. Mientras Estados Unidos y China se enfocan en crear modelos de IA más grandes, el Reino Unido se concentra en asegurar que la IA sea segura y ética.

**Qué pasó** – Londres, especialmente la zona de King’s Cross, se está convirtiendo en un centro importante para la seguridad de la IA. El gobierno británico apoya al AI Security Institute (AISI), que evalúa los nuevos modelos de IA de empresas como Anthropic y OpenAI para detectar riesgos y mejorar la seguridad. Londres también alberga laboratorios e universidades que estudian la ética y gobernanza de la IA.

**Impacto** – Este enfoque ayuda a prevenir usos peligrosos de la IA, como el hackeo o la creación de armas. La forma de trabajar de Londres ofrece una manera diferente de desarrollar IA, centrada en pruebas cuidadosas y responsabilidad. Las grandes empresas tecnológicas valoran el ambiente favorable del Reino Unido, que combina buena investigación con reglas equilibradas.

**Próximo paso** – El Reino Unido seguirá atrayendo empresas de IA para ampliar su trabajo en seguridad en Londres. Los investigadores esperan ampliar los esfuerzos de seguridad para incluir efectos sociales como la transparencia y la justicia. El gobierno y organizaciones como AISI continuarán probando y mejorando modelos de IA para reducir riesgos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Londres se está convirtiendo en un centro global clave para hacer que la IA sea más segura y ética, reuniendo expertos, empresas y apoyo gubernamental para guiar un desarrollo responsable de la IA.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing network of interconnected circuits and data streams forming the iconic outline of London’s King’s Cross station, surrounded by softly illuminated symbolic shields and locks representing AI safety and cybersecurity, with abstract silhouettes of floating AI model icons and softly glowing research lab buildings in the background, rendered in natural, muted tones.

articles.title: Universal gates from braiding and fusing anyons on quantum hardware
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10709-yA 54-qubit realization of non-Abelian S3 topological order demonstrates that combining anyon braiding with fusion enables universal topological quantum computation.
articles.content: A quantum computer requires the ability to store and manipulate information globally to protect against local noise. Topologically ordered phases1,2 offer two routes: encoding information in the ground-state subspace3 or in anyonic excitations1,4,5. The toric code1 exemplifies the first approach but does not intrinsically support a universal gate set. The latter—topological quantum computation—implements gates by braiding non-Abelian anyons6 around each other. However, the simplest non-Abelian generalizations of the toric code cannot achieve universality by braiding alone7,8,9. Here we demonstrate that anyon fusion, used as a computational primitive, renders these minimally non-Abelian topologically ordered states universal. We prepare a 54-qubit ground state of the quantum double of S 3 , the smallest non-Abelian group, on the H2 processor of Quantinuum. We encode logical information in the global fusion space of non-Abelian anyons, and by combining braiding with fusion, we realize a universal topological gate set and read-out, which we demonstrate by topologically preparing a magic state. This demonstrates that the S 3 topologically ordered state is scalably preparable, yet rich enough to support a universal gate set. More broadly, this work opens up new pathways for harnessing the intrinsic properties of quantum matter to manipulate quantum information.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in quantum computing by demonstrating universal topological quantum computation using anyon braiding and fusion on a 54-qubit quantum processor. This advancement has broad implications for scalable, noise-resistant quantum computers, which could revolutionize computing and benefit society at large. The article provides detailed context on the achievement and its impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists achieve universal quantum computing using anyons on 54-qubit processor
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers need ways to protect information from errors caused by tiny disturbances. One way is to use special states of matter called topological orders, which store information in a way that resists local noise. A method called topological quantum computation uses particles called anyons, which can be braided and fused to perform calculations.

**What happened** – Researchers used a 54-qubit quantum processor named H2 to create a special state called the quantum double of S3, the smallest non-Abelian group. They stored information in anyons and combined two operations: braiding (moving anyons around each other) and fusion (joining anyons). This combination allowed them to perform all the logical operations needed for universal quantum computing. They showed this by preparing a special “magic state” that proves their system can do complex calculations.

**Impact** – This is the first time that combining braiding and fusion of anyons on a real quantum computer has been shown to achieve universal quantum computing. It means that using these special particles can make quantum computers that are more stable and less affected by errors. This approach could help build bigger, more reliable quantum machines in the future.

**What's next step** – Scientists will work on scaling up this method to more qubits and more complex systems. They will also explore how to use these techniques to build practical quantum computers that can solve real-world problems faster than today’s machines.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Combining braiding and fusion of anyons on a 54-qubit processor has demonstrated a new way to perform universal, error-resistant quantum computing.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques réalisent un calcul quantique universel avec des anyons sur un processeur de 54 qubits
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques doivent protéger les informations contre les erreurs causées par de petites perturbations. Une méthode utilise des états spéciaux de la matière appelés ordres topologiques, qui stockent les données de façon résistante au bruit local. Le calcul quantique topologique utilise des particules appelées anyons, qui peuvent être tressées et fusionnées pour effectuer des calculs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont utilisé un processeur quantique de 54 qubits nommé H2 pour créer un état spécial appelé double quantique de S3, le plus petit groupe non-Abélien. Ils ont stocké l’information dans des anyons et combiné deux opérations : le tressage (déplacement des anyons les uns autour des autres) et la fusion (assemblage des anyons). Cette combinaison leur a permis de réaliser toutes les opérations logiques nécessaires pour un calcul quantique universel. Ils l’ont démontré en préparant un « état magique » spécial qui prouve la capacité de leur système à faire des calculs complexes.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que la combinaison du tressage et de la fusion d’anyons sur un ordinateur quantique réel permet un calcul quantique universel. Cela signifie que l’utilisation de ces particules spéciales peut rendre les ordinateurs quantiques plus stables et moins sensibles aux erreurs. Cette approche pourrait aider à construire des machines quantiques plus grandes et plus fiables à l’avenir.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont chercher à étendre cette méthode à plus de qubits et à des systèmes plus complexes. Ils vont aussi explorer comment utiliser ces techniques pour construire des ordinateurs quantiques pratiques capables de résoudre des problèmes réels plus rapidement que les machines actuelles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La combinaison du tressage et de la fusion d’anyons sur un processeur de 54 qubits a démontré une nouvelle façon de réaliser un calcul quantique universel et résistant aux erreurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos logran computación cuántica universal con anyones en procesador de 54 qubits
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas necesitan proteger la información de errores causados por pequeñas perturbaciones. Una forma es usar estados especiales de la materia llamados órdenes topológicos, que almacenan datos de manera que resisten el ruido local. La computación cuántica topológica usa partículas llamadas anyones, que se pueden trenzar y fusionar para hacer cálculos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores usaron un procesador cuántico de 54 qubits llamado H2 para crear un estado especial llamado doble cuántico de S3, el grupo no abeliano más pequeño. Guardaron la información en anyones y combinaron dos operaciones: trenzado (mover anyones alrededor de otros) y fusión (unir anyones). Esta combinación les permitió realizar todas las operaciones lógicas necesarias para una computación cuántica universal. Lo demostraron preparando un “estado mágico” especial que muestra que su sistema puede hacer cálculos complejos.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que combinar el trenzado y la fusión de anyones en una computadora cuántica real logra computación cuántica universal. Esto significa que usar estas partículas especiales puede hacer computadoras cuánticas más estables y menos propensas a errores. Este método podría ayudar a construir máquinas cuánticas más grandes y confiables en el futuro.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos trabajarán para ampliar este método a más qubits y sistemas más complejos. También explorarán cómo usar estas técnicas para construir computadoras cuánticas prácticas que puedan resolver problemas reales más rápido que las actuales.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Combinar el trenzado y la fusión de anyones en un procesador de 54 qubits ha demostrado una nueva forma de realizar computación cuántica universal y resistente a errores.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of intertwined glowing ribbons and delicate, luminous orbs connected by gentle threads, symbolizing the braiding and fusion of anyons within a softly glowing quantum lattice, rendered in natural, muted tones of blues, greens, and golds to evoke a sense of harmony and advanced quantum information flow.

articles.title: AI is set to completely transform cybersecurity — here’s how researchers must prepare
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02214-zAs automated tools accelerate both the discovery and the exploitation of software vulnerabilities, organizations must redesign their defences and workflows to keep pace.
articles.content:
For much of my career in computer science, I viewed the detection of software vulnerabilities as a craft. The best researchers combine technical skill with intuition: the ability to tell whether a software crash was a minor glitch or a sign of a more serious vulnerability. For decades, machines found bugs and humans decided which ones mattered. Now, with the advance of artificial intelligence, that is set to change drastically.

My research has focused mostly on building better fuzzers — automated tools that bombard software with millions of unexpected inputs to uncover bugs. Generating crash reports (detailed files that record the failures) was rarely the difficult part. The real challenge came afterwards. Someone still had to investigate each crash, determine whether it was exploitable, decide if it warranted disclosure, and work out how it could be fixed.

The uncritical adoption of AI in science is alarming — we urgently need guard rails

But AI is spurring a radical shift in how cybersecurity operates, turning vulnerability research into a scalable process that is powered by models, training data and computing power.

There are immense challenges as we navigate this path. Here, I sketch the contours of the emerging landscape and lay out open challenges.

AI systems can now do much more than generate code. Models that can reason, use tools and run experiments are increasingly capable of triaging software crashes, identifying root causes, assessing exploitability and even proposing fixes.

AI can review code that would otherwise go unexamined and shorten the path from bug discovery to a tested fix. Earlier this year, Mozilla, a technology firm in San Francisco, California, used a frontier AI model to uncover and patch 271 vulnerabilities in its Firefox browser for a single version release — a lot more than its existing tools and reviewers had found each month over the previous year.

AI is transforming the economy — understanding its impact requires both data and imagination

The sheer number of vulnerability reports that AI enables is stretching the review capacity of even experienced developers. The Linux kernel, the core open-source software that underpins many computer systems, relies on people reporting bugs. But in May 2026, the team that maintains the Linux kernel responded to a surge of duplicate AI-assisted reports by clarifying how such findings should be submitted. The lesson is not that software maintainers are failing, but that machine-generated reports can easily overwhelm processes built for a world in which vulnerabilities were discovered at human speed.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological advancement where AI is transforming cybersecurity by automating vulnerability detection and patching at scale. This has broad positive implications for software security globally, benefiting many users and organizations by improving safety and resilience against cyber threats. The article provides detailed context about the shift, examples of impact, and challenges, fulfilling criteria for good news.
articles.personality_title: AI is changing how we find and fix software bugs faster than ever
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, finding problems in software was mostly done by people using their skills and experience. They decided which bugs were serious and needed fixing. This process was slow because computers could find bugs, but humans had to check and fix them.

**What happened** – Now, artificial intelligence (AI) can do much more than just find bugs. It can understand which bugs are dangerous, suggest how to fix them, and even help test the fixes. For example, Mozilla used AI to find and fix 271 problems in their Firefox browser in one update, which is far more than before.

**Impact** – This is important because it means software can become safer much faster. AI can check more code than humans alone, helping protect computers and phones from hackers. But it also creates challenges, like handling the large number of bug reports AI produces, which can overwhelm the people who manage software updates.

**What's next step** – Researchers and software teams need to update their ways of working to handle AI’s speed. They must create better systems to review AI’s findings and decide what to fix first. This will help make sure AI’s help leads to stronger and more reliable software.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI is speeding up how we find and fix software problems, making technology safer but requiring new ways to manage this fast pace.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA révolutionne la détection et la correction des bugs logiciels plus rapidement que jamais
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, la recherche de problèmes dans les logiciels était surtout faite par des humains utilisant leurs compétences et leur expérience. Ils décidaient quels bugs étaient sérieux et devaient être corrigés. Ce processus était lent car les ordinateurs pouvaient trouver des bugs, mais les humains devaient les vérifier et les réparer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Aujourd’hui, l’intelligence artificielle (IA) peut faire bien plus que trouver des bugs. Elle peut comprendre quels bugs sont dangereux, proposer comment les corriger et même aider à tester les corrections. Par exemple, Mozilla a utilisé l’IA pour trouver et corriger 271 problèmes dans son navigateur Firefox en une seule mise à jour, bien plus qu’avant.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela signifie que les logiciels peuvent devenir plus sûrs beaucoup plus rapidement. L’IA peut vérifier plus de code qu’un humain seul, aidant à protéger les ordinateurs et les téléphones contre les pirates. Mais cela crée aussi des défis, comme gérer le grand nombre de rapports de bugs produits par l’IA, ce qui peut submerger les personnes qui gèrent les mises à jour.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs et les équipes de logiciels doivent adapter leurs méthodes pour suivre la rapidité de l’IA. Ils doivent créer de meilleurs systèmes pour examiner les découvertes de l’IA et décider quoi corriger en priorité. Cela permettra de s’assurer que l’aide de l’IA conduit à des logiciels plus solides et fiables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’IA accélère la découverte et la correction des problèmes logiciels, rendant la technologie plus sûre mais demandant de nouvelles façons de gérer ce rythme rapide.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA cambia cómo encontramos y arreglamos errores de software más rápido que nunca
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, encontrar problemas en el software lo hacían principalmente personas usando sus habilidades y experiencia. Ellos decidían qué errores eran graves y debían arreglarse. Este proceso era lento porque las computadoras podían encontrar errores, pero las personas tenían que revisarlos y corregirlos.

**Qué pasó** – Ahora, la inteligencia artificial (IA) puede hacer mucho más que encontrar errores. Puede entender cuáles son peligrosos, sugerir cómo arreglarlos e incluso ayudar a probar las soluciones. Por ejemplo, Mozilla usó IA para encontrar y arreglar 271 problemas en su navegador Firefox en una sola actualización, mucho más que antes.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque significa que el software puede volverse más seguro mucho más rápido. La IA puede revisar más código que una persona sola, ayudando a proteger computadoras y teléfonos contra hackers. Pero también crea desafíos, como manejar la gran cantidad de reportes de errores que produce la IA, lo que puede sobrecargar a las personas que gestionan las actualizaciones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores y equipos de software deben actualizar sus formas de trabajar para manejar la velocidad de la IA. Deben crear mejores sistemas para revisar los hallazgos de la IA y decidir qué arreglar primero. Esto ayudará a asegurar que la ayuda de la IA lleve a software más fuerte y confiable.

**Resumen en una frase** – La IA está acelerando cómo encontramos y arreglamos problemas de software, haciendo la tecnología más segura pero requiriendo nuevas formas de manejar este ritmo rápido.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate network of glowing circuit pathways shaped like an open book, with gentle streams of light flowing from a stylized, abstract AI brain at the center, illuminating and repairing fragmented code patterns represented by delicate, interlocking puzzle pieces, all rendered in soft, natural hues and fine brushstrokes evoking a sense of harmony and progress.

articles.title: Will making ‘replication studies’ easier to find help science self-correct?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02175-3Project on peer-review platform Pubpeer aims to make replication studies more visible.
articles.content:
Credit: bpawesome/Getty

The replication of experimental results is important for building credible science, but replication studies are often difficult to find. A team of researchers wants to change that. They have started putting replication studies on the post-publication peer-review platform PubPeer, together with links to the original studies.

There have been numerous attempts to replicate scientific experiments over the past decade or so, and many of these have not been able to reproduce the original results. But the inability to find these replication studies easily wastes research time and resources. If scientists aren't aware of a replication study, particularly one that was unsuccessful, they might cite the original research without knowing it has issues.

“If PubPeer can help people learn about replications, both successful and unsuccessful, that will speed the progress of science by facilitating scientific self-correction,” says Don Moore, a social psychologist at the University of California, Berkeley. A 2012 study by Moore on overconfidence1 was successfully replicated this year2.

The team behind the new project says it plans to create discussion threads on PubPeer for around 2,400 replication studies in batches of around 100 at a time. The replication studies are indexed in the FORRT Library of Reproduction and Replication Attempts (FLoRA), an initiative of the Framework for Open and Reproducible Research Training (FORRT), which is a community-driven effort to teach researchers about open science and reproducibility.

FORRT launched a database in 2024 to better catalogue replication studies. But the database’s creators at the Münster Center for Open Science in Germany say it’s still not easy to find replication studies because they are typically not linked to the original studies in many databases.

“Replications are generally less cited and less visible than original studies,” says Josefina Weinerova, a psychologist at Birkbeck, University of London, who is co-leading the PubPeer project.

Out of roughly 2,400 studies currently catalogued, almost 1000 have been successfully replicated independently, and 865 attempts at replication failed. For the remainder, researchers managed to replicate some of the outcomes.

Weinerova and her colleagues plan to inform the authors of all the papers that they have created threads for on PubPeer. Weinerova says that her team has already reached out to authors of the first batch of studies. Most didn’t reply, she says, and of the ones that did, none asked for comments not to be posted.

Because PubPeer is often used to flag potential issues or errors in papers, some researchers have complained about their papers being mentioned on PubPeer when no errors have been found.

But Weinerova says researchers should be pleased if studies confirming the validity of their work are highlighted on PubPeer. “Successful replication is still useful for other authors to know about,” she says. “While there may be perceived stigma with comments on PubPeer, there are also many comments that are not necessarily about pointing out major issues with papers.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful initiative to improve scientific transparency and reliability by making replication studies more accessible and visible. This has broad positive implications for the scientific community and society by facilitating self-correction and reducing wasted resources, thus enhancing the credibility and progress of science.
articles.personality_title: New project makes replication studies easier to find to improve science
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often try to repeat experiments to check if results are true. These are called replication studies. But these studies can be hard to find, which causes problems because researchers might miss important information about whether the original results are correct.

**What happened** – A group of researchers started adding replication studies to a website called PubPeer, where scientists can review and discuss research after it is published. They link these replication studies to the original experiments so people can easily find them. They are adding about 100 studies at a time, aiming to include around 2,400 studies in total.

**Impact** – Making replication studies easier to find helps scientists know which experiments have been checked and whether they worked or not. This can save time and effort by preventing researchers from repeating studies that have already been tested. It also helps science fix mistakes faster and become more reliable.

**What's next step** – The team will continue adding more replication studies to PubPeer and notify the authors of the original papers. They hope more researchers will join the discussion and use this resource to improve scientific knowledge.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Linking replication studies to original research on PubPeer helps science correct itself by making it easier to see which results have been confirmed or challenged.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau projet facilite l’accès aux études de réplication pour améliorer la science
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent souvent à répéter des expériences pour vérifier si les résultats sont corrects. Ces études s’appellent des études de réplication. Mais elles sont difficiles à trouver, ce qui pose problème car les chercheurs peuvent manquer des informations importantes sur la validité des résultats originaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un groupe de chercheurs a commencé à ajouter des études de réplication sur un site appelé PubPeer, où les scientifiques peuvent discuter et évaluer les recherches après leur publication. Ils lient ces études aux expériences originales pour les rendre faciles à trouver. Ils ajoutent environ 100 études à la fois, avec un objectif de 2 400 études au total.

**Impact** – Rendre les études de réplication plus accessibles aide les scientifiques à savoir quelles expériences ont été vérifiées et si elles ont réussi. Cela permet de gagner du temps en évitant de refaire des études déjà testées. Cela aide aussi la science à corriger ses erreurs plus rapidement et à devenir plus fiable.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe continuera d’ajouter des études de réplication sur PubPeer et informera les auteurs des articles originaux. Ils espèrent que davantage de chercheurs participeront aux discussions et utiliseront cette ressource pour améliorer les connaissances scientifiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Relier les études de réplication aux recherches originales sur PubPeer aide la science à s’auto-corriger en facilitant la visibilité des résultats confirmés ou remis en question.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo proyecto facilita encontrar estudios de replicación para mejorar la ciencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos suelen repetir experimentos para verificar si los resultados son correctos. Estos se llaman estudios de replicación. Pero estos estudios son difíciles de encontrar, lo que genera problemas porque los investigadores pueden perder información importante sobre la validez de los resultados originales.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de investigadores comenzó a agregar estudios de replicación en un sitio llamado PubPeer, donde los científicos pueden revisar y discutir investigaciones después de su publicación. Vinculan estos estudios con los originales para que sean fáciles de encontrar. Están agregando alrededor de 100 estudios a la vez, con la meta de incluir unos 2,400 en total.

**Impacto** – Facilitar el acceso a estudios de replicación ayuda a los científicos a saber qué experimentos han sido verificados y si funcionaron o no. Esto ahorra tiempo y esfuerzo evitando repetir estudios ya probados. También ayuda a que la ciencia corrija errores más rápido y sea más confiable.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo seguirá agregando más estudios de replicación a PubPeer y notificará a los autores de los artículos originales. Esperan que más investigadores participen en las discusiones y usen este recurso para mejorar el conocimiento científico.

**Resumen en una frase** – Vincular estudios de replicación con la investigación original en PubPeer ayuda a la ciencia a autocorregirse al facilitar ver qué resultados han sido confirmados o cuestionados.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an open book with glowing interconnected threads linking its pages to smaller, glowing companion books floating nearby, symbolizing original scientific studies and their replication studies, set against a soft, neutral background with gentle light illuminating the connections to represent transparency, collaboration, and the self-correcting nature of science.
